coredata.dc:title: Tuning photovoltaic performance of co-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Peng
Wu, Qiulan
Li, Zhiwei
coredata.dc:description: The photovoltaic performance of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) faces instability due to structural irregularities. To understand optoelectronic properties, this study employs density functional theory (DFT) to investigate nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs) co-doped with sulfur (N,S) and phosphorous (N,P). Co-doping with N and S or N and P reduces electron-hole pair separation, increasing thermal loss in GQDs with graphitic N functionality. N,S co-doping heightens surface defect likelihood, while N,P co-doping diminishes it. The chemical reactivity of NGQDs is influenced by polar solvent choice, unlike excited-state properties of GQDs doped with other elements. NGQDs with graphitic and pyrrolic N functionalities outperform co-doped GQDs in photovoltaic performance due to enhanced parameters. This study guides rational design of electronic devices using GQD materials.

head.ce:title: Tuning photovoltaic performance of co-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of New Materials and Shoes & Clothing Engineering, Liming Vocational University, Quanzhou 362000, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photovoltaic performance of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) faces instability due to structural irregularities. To understand optoelectronic properties, this study employs density functional theory (DFT) to investigate nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs) co-doped with sulfur (N,S) and phosphorous (N,P). Co-doping with N and S or N and P reduces electron-hole pair separation, increasing thermal loss in GQDs with graphitic N functionality. N,S co-doping heightens surface defect likelihood, while N,P co-doping diminishes it. The chemical reactivity of NGQDs is influenced by polar solvent choice, unlike excited-state properties of GQDs doped with other elements. NGQDs with graphitic and pyrrolic N functionalities outperform co-doped GQDs in photovoltaic performance due to enhanced parameters. This study guides rational design of electronic devices using GQD materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their customizable electronic structures and non-toxicity, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) emerge as highly promising materials for solar applications. The photovoltaic performance of GQDs, encompassing energy harvesting and solar energy conversion efficiency, relies significantly on the elemental constituents within GQDs. However, the mechanisms of excited-state charge transfer for GQDs remain a subject of controversy. It is generally accepted that doping and surface chemistry play crucial roles in shaping the photophysical properties of GQDs. Heteroatom doping, in particular, alters the electronic structures of GQDs, offering a viable method to fine-tune their optoelectronic properties. For instance, the research by Feng et al. highlights that B/P/S doping introduces new electrical and optical characteristics to GQDs [1]. Similarly, Guo et al. report that introducing various ions or groups to GQDs can alter their electrocatalytic activity [2]. Feng et al. delved into the effects of B doping configurations on the optoelectronic properties of GQDs, revealing that both the B-dopant and its oxidized form facilitate charge transfer during photoexcitation [3].

GQDs have undergone doping with various atoms to modify their excited-state properties, specifically the dynamics of non-radiative electron-hole recombination crucial for photovoltaic applications. Reduced non-radiative electron-hole recombination opens the possibility of separating electron-hole pairs, minimizing losses in carrier thermalization. The lower the losses in carrier thermalization, the more efficiently photons can convert into charge carriers, collectible for specific photovoltaic applications. To delve into the electrical and optical properties of GQDs, researchers explored various heteroatom dopants. For instance, Li et al. utilized a hydrothermal approach to prepare sulfur-doped GQDs (SGQDs), revealing that the addition of sulfur atoms to the carbon skeleton induces unique electronic states between π- and π*-energy levels, resulting in a series of emission peaks [4]. Another study by Li et al. synthesized phosphorus-doped GQDs (PGQDs) through an electrochemical method, intending to use them as radical scavengers [5]. Hai et al. adopted an acid-free one-pot microwave method to prepare boron-doped GQDs (BGQDs) [6], resulting in BGQDs with a fluorescent quantum yield of 21.1 %. These pioneering works establish the groundwork for developing GQD materials with minimized non-radiative electron-hole recombination rates, ultimately enhancing photovoltaic performance.

Studying the impact of doping is crucial for developing strategies to tailor the properties of materials for improved performance, as demonstrated by recent works showing enhancements via doping [7–10]. Nitrogen (N) is a commonly used dopant to modify the excited-state properties of GQDs. Because of its similar size to carbon (C) atoms, it causes less strain on the graphene lattice. There are three doping functionalities for introducing N atoms into the graphene lattice: graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N. The excited-state properties of GQDs depend on these three N functionalities. Graphitic N, according to Das, facilitates excited-state charge transfer, resulting in a photoluminescence quantum yield of around 34 % [11]. On the other hand, Noor's research indicates that pyrrolic N increases the quantum yield in white light emission [12]. Permatasari's study explores the effects of these three N functionalities on the photoluminescence (PL) of GQDs [13]. Interestingly, they found that pyridinic N increases the PL efficiency, while pyrrolic N decreases it. However, Permatasari's findings contradict Noor's results regarding the role of pyrrolic N in modulating the PL intensity of N-doped GQDs (NGQDs). This suggests that the relationship between N functionalities and the optical properties of GQDs is not yet fully understood. Therefore, further investigation is necessary to understand the relationship between N functionality and the optoelectronic properties of GQDs.

Co-doping is a technique employed to tailor the electronic structures and excited state properties of GQDs. In comparison to single doping, co-doping introduces greater heterogeneity to GQDs, resulting in novel photophysical properties. The specific configurations of dual dopants play a crucial role in determining the photovoltaic performance of GQDs. The configurations of GQDs can be manipulated by adjusting the reaction temperature. For instance, temperatures ranging from 600 to 800℃ facilitate the conversion of pyrrolic N and pyridinic N to graphitic N [14]. This temperature adjustment also impacts the S content in N,S co-doped GQDs, with an increase observed as the temperature rises [15]. Conversely, in P,N co-doped GQDs, the content of P and N decreases [16]. These co-doping techniques have distinct effects on the properties of GQDs. S,N co-doping, for example, enhances the absorption of solar energy by shifting the primary absorption region to 550–595 nm [17]. Similarly, P,N co-doped GQDs improve the photocatalytic ability of TiO2 for dye degradation [18]. Despite previous research demonstrating the alterability of co-doping configurations by temperature, a comprehensive understanding of their impact on the excited-state properties of GQDs is lacking. Researchers must elucidate this relationship to develop an effective synthetic method for producing GQDs with desired properties.

Studying the effects of doping configurations on the excited state properties of GQDs through optical spectroscopy poses a challenge. In contrast, theoretical methods prove effective in exploring the relationship between GQD structures and properties. For instance, density functional theory (DFT) calculations [19] investigate nonradiative electron-hole recombination caused by various N functionalities. The study reveals that graphitic and pyridinic N induce midgap states, accelerating nonradiative electron-hole recombination. In a similar vein, Niu et al. employ DFT simulations to explore the electrical structures of NGQDs with different N doping positions [20]. Their findings indicate that central N doping leads to charge trap states in GQDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching. While previous studies predominantly focus on the impact of single doping on electronic and optical properties of GQDs [13,21–24], limited attention has been given to the influence of dual-atom doping, specifically the co-doping effect, on the excited state dynamics of GQDs [1,25]. Hence, further investigation into the impact of co-doping configurations on the excited-state properties of GQDs is of utmost importance.

The electronic structures and properties of GQDs co-doped with N, S, and N, P are examined in this study using DFT and TDDFT simulations. The primary goal is to assess the impact of three different nitrogen functionalities—pyridine N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N—on the photovoltaic performance of these co-doped GQDs. This assessment encompasses energy conversion and photoelectric performance. The insights gained from this research have significant implications for the synthesis of GQDs in various applications, including solar cells [26,27], light-emitting diodes [28,29], and water splitting [30,31].

N,S, and N,P co-doped GQDs involve two types of co-dopants: P and S. The N dopant introduces graphitic N (Ng), pyridinic N (Nh), and pyrrolic N (Np) functionalities into the graphene lattice of the GQDs (see Fig. 1 ). X-ray spectroscopy reveals that P and S atoms tend to oxidize at the GQD edges, forming C–X = OC structures (X = P and S) [32,33]. For clarity, we use “R” for the N functionality and “X” for the P and S atoms. Hence, we refer to N-doped GQDs as GQD–R and N,X co-doped GQDs as GQD–RXC2O. To maintain the planetary structure of the GQD, heteroatoms are strategically placed only at the GQD edges, as adding them to the basal plane can cause structural defects and deformations [24,33].

The geometries of all GQDs were optimized using the DFT method without symmetry constraints. We employed the PBE1PBE functional, known for its accurate description of electronic and optical properties in GQD materials [19,34], for all calculations. In the pursuit of optimization, we implement precise guidelines to ensure convergence, considering energy and gradient thresholds. Specifically, we set the maximum force and root mean square (RMS) force at 0.00045 Hartree/Bohr and 0.0003 Hartree/Bohr, respectively. Additionally, we limit the maximum displacement and RMS displacement to 0.0018 Bohr and 0.0012 Bohr, respectively. To validate the accuracy of the selected functionals, we conducted a comparison of the band gaps of pristine GQDs (C46H18) calculated using seven different functionals: PBE, TPSS, B3LYP, CAMB3LYP, PBE1PBE, M062X and wb97x (refer to supplemental Figure S1). Figure S1 shows band gaps of pristine GQD calculated with functionals having different percentages of exact exchange, revealing an overall trend of increasing band gap with higher exact exchange – PBE and TPSS with no exact exchange give very small < 1 eV gaps, B3LYP and PBE1PBE with modest exact exchange provide gaps of 0.4–0.5 eV in good agreement with tight-binding calculations (0.451 eV [35]), while even higher exact exchange in CAM-B3LYP, wb97x and M06-2X leads to much wider 2–7 eV gaps. This sensitivity highlights the role of exchange treatment in predicting excitation properties of graphene-based materials, for which functionals with modest exact exchange like PBE1PBE used in this study seem to give the most accurate band gaps closely aligned with other calculations [34,36,37,38]. Furthermore, previous studies have demonstrated that the band gaps of GQDs, calculated using the tight-binding method, exhibit significant agreement with experimental results [39–42]. Thus, the tight-binding results serve as suitable reference values for benchmarking purposes. To further validate the selected functional, we assessed the absorption spectra of larger-sized GQDs (C145H46O2, depicted in Figure S2), which have been experimentally synthesized [41]. The experimentally determined absorption maxima is 542 nm [41]. The absorption maxima calculated with different functionals, including PBE, TPSS, CAM-B3LYP, M06-2X, wb97x, PBE1PBE, and B3LYP, are 674 nm, 597 nm, 450 nm, 459 nm, 359 nm, 537 nm, and 561 nm, respectively. The PBE1PBE and B3LYP functionals exhibit absorption maxima that are blue- and red-shifted compared to the experimental value, respectively, though they are much closer than the other functionals tested. Notably, the PBE1PBE functional provides an absorption maxima much closer to the measured value than the B3LYP functional. In conclusion, both Figure S1 and Figure S2 validate the use of PBE1PBE for accurately modeling the electronic structure of GQDs. Toluene served as the solvent, and the polarizable continuum model [42] was applied for modeling. We chose toluene as the solvent for our study due to its extensive use in comparable DFT studies [20,44–46], enabling direct comparisons of findings. Furthermore, toluene's efficient ability to dissolve GQDs, coupled with its low reactivity that minimizes interference in investigating photophysical properties, renders it a fitting and prudent choice. The calculation involved determining 40 excited states through Time-Dependent DFT (TDDFT). Both DFT and TDDFT calculations utilized the BDF software package [46–51]. The Multiwfn software package [51] was employed to calculate natural transition orbitals (NTOs) and charge transfer lengths.

The band gap of pristine GQD (C46H18, Fig. 2 ) was calculated to be 0.436 eV, surpassing the previously reported value of 0.271 eV [52]. Our findings indicate a higher value, influenced by variations in functional and solvent conditions. Unlike the earlier theoretical calculation conducted in a vacuum with the B3LYP functional, the solvent effect stabilizes the surface state of the Quantum Dots (QDs), leading to an increase in their band gaps [53]. Additionally, an increase in exact exchange also contributes to the band gap opening [54]. To ensure the accuracy of the basis set, we evaluate the band gap of pyrene (C16H10) using three different basis sets: 3-21G, 6-31G*, and 6-311G** in toluene (see Supplemental Table S1). The computed band gaps are 4.25 eV (3-21G), 4.17 eV (6-31G*), and 4.16 eV (6-311G**), with a tendency consistent with the previous result [52]. This indicates that increasing the basis set size from 6 to 31G* to 6-311G** does not significantly alter the band gap of pristine GQD. Therefore, 6-31G* is deemed acceptable for calculating the electronic structure of GQDs.

To assess the stability of GQDs with single and co-doping, we calculate the cohesive energy (δ). This value crucially determines the likelihood of surface defects, including single-vacancy, double vacancy, and Stone-Wales defect [55], occurring within the GQDs. A smaller δ indicates a higher difficulty for surface defects to form within the GQDs. Additionally, we evaluate the photoelectric performance of both single- and co-doped GQDs by calculating three key parameters: chemical hardness (η), chemical potential (μ), and electro-donating power (
ω
-
). In nanomaterials, smaller values of μ and
ω
-
are preferred as they facilitate electron transfer [56–59]. The η-value represents the reactivity of a molecule and is equivalent to the band gap. A smaller η-value signifies easier polarization, enabling the exchange of charges with the environment and ultimately enhancing reactivity. Therefore, smaller values of η, μ, and
ω
-
contribute to an improved photoelectric performance. For detailed calculations of η, μ,
ω
-
, and δ, please refer to Section I of the supplementary materials.

The performance of a light sensitizer in photovoltaic applications is heavily influenced by its ability to efficiently convert incoming photons into charge carriers. This efficiency, also known as energy conversion efficiency (η), is a key factor in determining the overall effectiveness of the sensitizer. A higher value of η indicates greater success in converting photons into electrons, enhancing the efficiency of the photovoltaic device. According to a previous study [59], η correlates with open-circuit voltage (Voc), short-circuit current (Jsc), and fill factor (FF), and is given by the following equation [60]:

Overall, equations (1)-(8) demonstrate that improving the photovoltaic performance of GQDs involves increasing Voc, FF, and LHE values [63,64].

Fig. 2 illustrates the different doping configurations of GQDs and their corresponding optimized structures. The calculated CC bond length of the carbon skeleton in GQDs is 1.42 Å, consistent with findings from previous theoretical investigations [53,54]. In the doped GQDs, the SC, PC, PO, and SO bond lengths measure 1.62 Å, 1.69 Å, 1.49 Å, and 1.47 Å, respectively. These measurements align closely with both theoretical predictions and results from X-ray spectroscopic studies [69–73].

In contrast, the η of GQD–Np decreases after co-doping with SC2O and PC2O, indicating that the S and P co-dopants increase the chemical reactivity of NGQDs with Np functionality. Moreover, the order of η-values of GQD–NpSC2O and GQD–NpPC2O differs from that of GQD–SC2O and GQD–PC2O, indicating that Np functionality plays a more important role in the chemical hardness of N,S and N,P co-doped GQDs than Nh doping. The trend of η-values for GQD–NgSC2O and GQD–NgPC2O depends on the S and P co-dopants. The η of GQD–NgSC2O is smaller than that of GQD–Ng, whereas the η of GQD–NgPC2O is larger than that of GQD–Ng. However, the η-values of both GQD–NgPC2O and GQD–NgSC2O decrease compared with GQD–Ng in polar solvents (Supplementary Table S2), consistent with the GQD–NgPC2O and GQD–NgSC2O. This suggests that the polar solvent reduces the influence of the S and P dopants on the chemical reactivity of the NGQDs with graphitic N functionality. The differences in η-values with respect to the N functionalities (Ng and Np) for N,S and N,P co-doped GQDs are due to the combined effect of dual doping on the rectification of the electronic structure of the GQDs.

The μ values of NGQDs follow the order GQD–Nh < GQD–Np < GQD–Ng. GQDs with Nh functionality exhibit the smallest μ value compared to other NGQDs because the pyridinic N donates two pi electrons to the conjugated π–system of the GQDs, enhancing their electron adsorption ability [1]. While pyrrolic N donates one pi electron to the conjugated π–system of the graphene lattice, it also forms sp3 hybridization within the carbon skeleton of GQDs. This results in a decreased delocalization of pi electrons in GQD–Np, leading to a larger μ value than pristine GQD. Graphitic N has a negligible effect on the pi electrons of GQDs due to the formation of sp2 hybridization with neighboring carbon atoms. However, because of its stronger electronegativity, graphitic N withdraws electrons from neighboring carbon atoms, causing a localization effect within the carbon skeleton [19] and a decrease in the μ of GQD–Ng. Overall, μ decreases for N,P, and N,S co-doped GQDs compared to NGQDs (i.e., GQD–Ng, GQD–Np, and GQD–Nh), suggesting that P/S co-dopants enhance the electron-attracting ability of NGQDs. Specifically, for NGQDs with Ng and Np functionalities, co-doping with P and S lowers their μ values. On the other hand, for NGQDs with Nh functionality, S co-doping decreases the μ value in nonpolar solvents while increasing it in polar solvents. (Refer to Supplementary Table S2 for details).

In comparison, the
ω
-
of GQD–Ng and GQD–Np are smaller than those of GQD–Nh, suggesting that the Ng and Np functionalities increase the ability of NGQDs to donate electrons. The
ω
-
increases after P/S co-doping, indicating that the P/S co-dopant decreases the ability of NGQDs with Np functionality to donate electrons. Conversely, for NGQDs with Nh functionality, the
ω
-
decreases after P/S co-doping, indicative of a boosted electron-donating ability with P/S co-dopant. P/S co-doping has different effects on the overall photoelectric performance of GQDs, depending on the N doping configuration. In the case of Ng functionality, P co-doping provides better electron transfer capability; for Nh functionality, S co-doping provides better electron transfer capability; for NGQDs with Np functionality, P co-doping provides better electron transfer capability. This discrepancy reflects the dual doping effect on the electronic structures of GQDs and can be utilized to modify the optoelectronic properties of GQDs.

The calculated values of δ for doped GQDs are around –7.4 eV, consistent with a previous theoretical report [1]. The stability of GQDs can be influenced by different doping elements. Introducing Np/Nh doping results in decreased δ values for GQD–Np and GQD–Nh compared to pristine GQD, indicating improved stability. Conversely, the presence of GQD–Ng, GQD–PC2O, and GQD–SC2O leads to larger δ values, suggesting decreased stability. It is noteworthy that the introduction of N,S co-doping does not enhance structural stability, irrespective of the N functionalities, as indicated by larger δ values compared to pristine GQD. This implies that Ng/P/S doping or N,S co-doping increases the likelihood of surface defects, resulting in reduced photoelectric performance. However, the inclusion of N,P co-doping significantly enhances the structural stability of GQDs with Np/Nh functionalities, evidenced by smaller δ values. Therefore, P co-doping plays a crucial role in preventing the formation of surface defects in GQDs with Np/Nh functionalities. The influence of a specific doping configuration in combination with a particular type of surface defect on the photoelectric performance of GQDs is not the main focus of this work and will be explored in the next study. Furthermore, the polar solvent slightly increases the δ values of the GQDs compared with the nonpolar solvent (Table 1 and supplementary Table S2), suggesting that surface defects form more easily in the polar solvent than in the nonpolar solvent.

Overall, the N,P co-doped GQDs with Np functionality (GQD–NpPC2O) demonstrate a smaller chemical potential and electron-donating power compared to other single and co-doped GQDs. Consequently, GQD–NpPC2O exhibits enhanced charge transfer capability and improved photoelectric performance. Moreover, it demonstrates superior structural stability compared to the majority of other co-doped GQDs in the study.

Fig. 3 shows the projected density of states (PDOS) for single- and co-doped GQDs. The Fermi level of GQD–SC2O is about –2.2 eV (Fig. 3 (k)). However, the Fermi levels of GQD–NgSC2O, GQD–NhSC2O, and GQD–NpSC2O are about –4 eV (Fig. 3 (c), (f), and (l)), which is closer to the Fermi level of GQD–Ng, GQD–Nh, and GQD–Np (Fig. 3 (a), (d), and (g)), indicating that the N functionality plays a more crucial role in determining the frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) of N,S co-doped GQDs and that S dopant acts as a perturbation of FMOs in N,S co-doped GQDs, as seen in their PDOS (Fig. 3 (c), (f) and (i)). In contrast, P co-doping has no significant effect on the FMOs of NGQDs, as shown by the similar PDOS between NGQDs and N,P co-doped GQDs (Fig. 3 (a), (b), (d), (e), (g), and (h)). Be noted, increasing the polar solvent stabilizes the surface states of GQDs, pushing the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) into lower energy levels [53].

The polarity of the solvent generally has a limited impact on the absorption spectra of single- and co-doped GQDs, with the exception of GQD–NgPC2O (see Fig. 4 and Supplementary Figure S3). The insensitivity of GQDs' absorption spectra to solvent polarity results from their rigid planar structure and the delocalized pi-electron system, rendering them less susceptible to solvent-induced perturbations compared to other molecular systems [73–76]. Solvent molecules encounter difficulty disrupting sp2 hybridized carbon bonding due to this structural stability, thereby restricting solvent effects on GQDs. However, the combined effects of graphitic N and P co-doping may alter the electronic structure and surface chemistry of GQDs, making them more responsive to solvent effects. Specifically, the configuration of Ng and P dopants could create localized states or distort symmetry, influencing the distribution of charges and dipole moments in the GQD. This modification enhances solute–solvent interactions and stabilizes certain excited states differently in polar versus nonpolar solvents. For instance, in NGQDs with Ng functionality, P co-doping amplifies the main absorption peak intensity in nonpolar solvents (Fig. 4(b)), while having minimal impact in polar solvents (Supplementary Figure S3(b), (e), (h)). This discrepancy suggests that the P co-dopant modifies the optical transitions of NGQDs in nonpolar solvents while mitigating its influence on optical transitions in polar solvents. On the contrary, for GQD–NhPC2O and GQD–NpPC2O, the P co-dopant does not significantly affect optical transitions, as evidenced by the similar absorption profiles between NGQDs and N,P co-doped GQDs (Fig. 4(d), (e), (g), and (h)).

However, the S co-dopant induces a significant alteration in the electron-hole recombination dynamics of NGQDs, consequently affecting the absorption intensity. Specifically, in the case of GQD–NhSC2O, the primary absorption peak aligns with that of GQD–SC2O, signifying that sulfur doping, rather than nitrogen doping, predominantly influences the optical transition of GQD–NhSC2O. In contrast, for GQD–NpSC2O and GQD–NgSC2O, the main absorption peaks in these variants are heightened compared to GQD–Np, GQD–Nh, and GQD–SC2O. This implies that the combined impact of sulfur and nitrogen dopants significantly modifies the electron-hole recombination dynamics, consequently influencing the optical transitions in GQD–NpSC2O and GQD–NgSC2O.

According to the Kasha rule, the relaxation between excited states is usually faster than that of the lowest excited state due to smaller energy gaps [76]. Therefore, the photovoltaic performance of an electronic device typically depends on the recombination of electron-hole pairs in the lowest excited state of a light sensitizer. Understanding the distribution and separation of these pairs offers valuable insights for designing the structure of the light sensitizer for specific applications. The charge transfer length (R) characterizes the electron-hole separation, where a smaller charge transfer length indicates a more efficient nonradiative recombination, and vice versa. Table 2 illustrates a significant hole density at the edges of pristine GQD, while the electron density is delocalized over the carbon skeleton of pristine GQD, consistent with earlier theoretical reports [52].

The pristine GQD exhibits a R of 2.21 Å, surpassing the 2 Å threshold and indicating a charge transfer excitation in the lowest excited state [77]. Triangular GQDs, with a significantly larger R compared to hexagonal counterparts, demonstrate inefficient nonradiative decay in their lowest excited state, emphasizing the impact of charge transfer length [19]. Specifically, GQD–Ng and GQD–Nh display higher R values than pristine GQDs, while GQD–Np shows a smaller R, aligning with experimental reports [13,21]. Conversely, GQD–SC2O and GQD–PC2O exhibit longer R values than pristine GQDs. Notably, GQD–NgPC2O and GQD–NgSC2O showcase a drastic reduction in R compared to GQD–Ng, GQD–PC2O, and GQD–SC2O, indicating nonradiative decay dominance in optical transitions for these compounds. This reduction, attributed to Ng,P and Ng,S co-doping, leads to a decreased carrier density at the GQD's LUMO level, impacting photovoltaic performance negatively. In the case of GQD–NhPC2O, N,P co-doping increases R, enhancing the quantum yield of photogenerated charges, while N,S co-doping in GQD–NhSC2O decreases R, resulting in reduced quantum yield due to heat loss. Additionally, R values for GQD–NpPC2O and GQD–NpSC2O increase compared to GQD–PC2O, GQD–SC2O, and GQD–Np, indicating that Np,S and Np,P co-doping effectively reduces non-radiative electron-hole recombination in GQDs.

The photovoltaic performance of an electrical device relies primarily on two key parameters: Voc and Jsc. Diminished Voc and Jsc contribute to low photovoltaic performance, consequently lowering the energy conversion efficiency of the solar device [78]. Various electrical structures of light sensitizers are linked to Voc and Jsc. Hence, it is imperative to investigate the impact of different GQD doping configurations on solar energy conversion.

Table 3 displays the energy conversion efficiencies of GQD–Ng and GQD–Np, with GQD–Ng exhibiting the highest efficiency at 3.2 %, followed by GQD–Np at 2.24 %. The efficiency (η) of N/S and N/P co-doped GQDs ranges from 0.04 to 1.5 %, indicating that S and P co-dopants do not significantly enhance the η of NGQDs. In the case of GQD–Nh, although the LHE is higher than that of GQD–Ng and GQD–Np, the Jsc is smaller due to more optically permissible transitions in GQD–Ng and GQD–Np than in GQD–Nh. Furthermore, while the Jsc values of GQD–NpPC2O and GQD–NpSC2O surpass those of GQD–Np, their Voc and FF are smaller, resulting in a lower overall η. In the case of GQD–Nh, P and S co-dopants have distinct effects on Voc and FF. The former decreases Voc and FF, while the latter increases them. Particularly, the S co-dopant significantly reduces Jsc due to the smaller LHE. The efficiency trend of GQD–SC2O and GQD–PC2O aligns with that of GQD–NhSC2O and GQD–NhPC2O, indicating the dominant role of S and P co-dopants in the energy conversion of GQDs. Conversely, the efficiency trend of GQD–SC2O and GQD–PC2O opposes that of N/P and N/S co-doped GQDs with Np and Ng functionalities, suggesting an interaction between N doping and P/S co-doping in determining the energy conversion processes of GQDs.

In summary, NGQDs with Ng and Np functionalities exhibit superior energy conversion efficiencies compared to other GQDs, highlighting the beneficial impact of N doping alone for photovoltaic applications. Conversely, N/S and N/P co-doping does not significantly enhance the photovoltaic performance of electronic devices utilizing GQDs.

In this investigation, we explore the impact of S/P co-doping on the photoelectric and photovoltaic capabilities of GQDs. The introduction of S and P co-dopants has distinct effects on the photoelectric performance of NGQDs, influenced by the type of N functionalities present. Specifically, N,P co-doped GQDs with Nh functionalities exhibit superior overall photoelectric performance compared to other doped GQDs.

The FMOs of N,S co-doped GQDs are primarily influenced by the N functionality, although the presence of S as a co-dopant hinders the process. The absorption spectra of single- and co-doped GQDs are minimally affected by solvent polarity, except for N,P co-doped GQDs with graphitic N functionality. Furthermore, the combination of graphitic N and P greatly reduces the separation distance between electrons and holes, resulting in very short charge transfer lengths.

Additionally, NGQDs with graphitic N functionality demonstrate exceptional photovoltaic performance compared to other single- or co-doped GQDs. The incorporation of P and S as co-dopants adversely impacts the efficiency of energy conversion in NGQDs functionalized with graphitic nitrogen (N), resulting in subpar performance in photovoltaic uses. This study provides valuable insights into the effects of different doping configurations on the photoelectric and photovoltaic properties of GQDs co-doped with N,P and N,S, assisting in the strategic advancement of electronic devices incorporating GQDs.
